mnemonics.title: Disk Access Time Formula
mnemonics.content:
SAT = ST + RL + TT
(Search: Seek Time + Rotational Latency + Transfer Time)

mnemonics.title: RAID Levels Memory Aid
mnemonics.content:
RAID 0 = Zero redundancy (Striping)
RAID 1 = One mirror (Mirroring)
RAID 5 = Parity (Striping with distributed parity)
Remember: 0=Striped, 1=Mirrored, 5=Parity protected

acronyms.usage: Disk scheduling algorithm that processes requests in order of arrival
Disk scheduling that services request closest to current head position
Disk scheduling moving arm from one end to other, reversing direction
Disk scheduling that services in one direction then jumps back to start
Storage device using flash memory instead of magnetic platters
Type of flash memory used in SSDs (named after its logic gate structure)
Command letting OS tell SSD which blocks are no longer in use
Technology combining multiple disks for performance and/or redundancy

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a vinyl record with concentric circles (tracks). Now stack multiple records (platters) on a single turntable. The arm moves like a tonearm across these circles to read/write data.

Think of an elevator in a building going from bottom to top, picking up passengers (requests) at each floor, then going back down. C-SCAN is like an elevator that goes up, then teleports back to bottom (doesn't pick up passengers on return).

Imagine a grid of light bulbs (cells). Each bulb can be turned on/off (representing bits). Over time, some bulbs burn out. Wear leveling is like rotating which bulbs you use so all burn out at the same rate.
Think of a stack of paper with holes punched in the same position - when you put a pencil through, it passes through all papers at the same radial distance. That's a cylinder.

analogies.analogy: If RAM is like a notepad (fast to write, but you lose it when you leave), a magnetic disk is like a filing cabinet (slower to access, but keeps everything safe when power is off).

Imagine you have a team of workers and only ask one person to do all the writing. That person will get tired quickly. Wear leveling is like rotating the work among all workers so no one gets exhausted too fast.
It's like cleaning a messy desk. You gather the important papers (valid data), move them to a clean area, and throw away the scrap paper (invalid data) to free up space for new work.
Think of it like a puzzle where pieces are spread across friends. If one friend loses their pieces, the rest can recreate what that friend had using clues (parity) each of them kept.
